output.final_deliverable:
I searched the web (results merged below) for three authoritative sources (government, news outlet, research institution) reporting San Francisco’s current population and compiled available citations. The merged search results provide source URLs and some metadata, but the actual numeric population figures and the exact data dates are not present in the merged results. Therefore I cannot report the numeric population values from those pages yet. Below I list each source found in the merged results, what was extracted from the search output, what is missing, and the direct URL for you to review or for me to visit next.

1) Government — U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts (San Francisco city, California)
   - Citation URL: https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/sanfranciscocitycalifornia/INC110223
   - What the merged search output shows: the QuickFacts page was identified and its description states QuickFacts derives data from Population Estimates, the American Community Survey, Census counts, etc. (this is visible in the search snippet).
   - What is missing from merged results: the specific population figure and the reference date (e.g., "as of July 1, 2023" or the Census April 1, 2020 count) were not extracted in the merged results. I do not have the exact numeric population or the exact date from that page in the merged results.
   - Methodology note (from the search snippet): QuickFacts compiles population estimates, ACS, and census data (see the QuickFacts page description) (https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/sanfranciscocitycalifornia/INC110223).

2) News outlet — San Francisco Chronicle
   - Citation URL: https://www.sfchronicle.com/sf/article/s-f-population-falls-to-lowest-level-in-over-a-17744210.php
   - What the merged search output shows: an SF Chronicle article titled "San Francisco population falls to lowest level in over a decade" (by Roland Li, Yuri Avila), with an update date shown in the search snippet: Jan 27, 2023.
   - What is missing from merged results: the article text in the merged results does not include the numeric population figure(s) quoted in the story nor the specific data date(s) the article used. I cannot extract the population number from the merged results alone.
   - Methodology note: news stories typically cite official estimates or Census/DOF figures; the article date from the snippet is Jan 27, 2023 (https://www.sfchronicle.com/sf/article/s-f-population-falls-to-lowest-level-in-over-a-17744210.php).

3) Research institution — (no direct result found in merged search results)
   - What the merged search output shows: I searched for research institutions (PPIC, Brookings, PRB, UC Berkeley) but the merged results returned no specific research-institution page with a San Francisco population figure.
   - What is missing: a research-institution source URL, the population figure, and the data date. No research-institution population estimate was present in the merged results.

Discrepancies and explanation:
- I cannot identify numeric discrepancies between the sources because the merged search results do not include the actual population numbers or their reference dates. Without those extracted figures I cannot compare them.
- General explanation (from the search/snippets and the plan): when different sources report different population numbers for the same place, common reasons are: different reference dates (Census 2020 count vs. July 1 annual estimates), different geography definitions (city limits vs. metropolitan area), and different methodologies (decennial census counts vs. annual population estimates vs. ACS sample-based estimates). The QuickFacts description notes it draws from population estimates and ACS and census data, which explains typical methodological differences (https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/sanfranciscocitycalifornia/INC110223).

Which figure is most likely accurate and why:
- Based on the types of sources found, an official government estimate (for example, the U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts page) is the most authoritative single source for an official city population estimate because it is produced by the federal statistical agency using standardized methods and is explicitly described as deriving from population estimates, ACS, and census counts (https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/sanfranciscocitycalifornia/INC110223).
- A state demographer (California Department of Finance) or the City & County of San Francisco planning or demographic unit would be similarly authoritative; none of those exact pages with numeric values were returned or extracted in the merged results I used here.

Next step I recommend (I can do this now if you want): visit each of the three target pages (the Census QuickFacts page above; the SF Chronicle article above; and a research-institution page such as PPIC, Brookings, or PRB) and extract the exact population figure(s) and the explicit data date(s). If you want, I will follow up immediately to visit those URLs and return the numeric population figures, dates, and direct citations for each source and then analyze discrepancies numerically.

Summary of what is missing from the merged results: the exact numeric population figures and the explicit data dates for all three sources (government, news, research) are not present in the merged search outputs. I can fetch those precise values and complete the comparison on your go-ahead.
output.action_summary: Merged search results located the U.S. Census QuickFacts page for San Francisco and an SF Chronicle article (Jan 27, 2023) and showed that research-institution results were not found in the merged outputs. However, the merged results do not include the numeric population figures or explicit data dates. I recommend (and can now) visit the Census QuickFacts page, the SF Chronicle article, and a selected research-institution page (PPIC/PRB/Brookings/UC Berkeley) to extract the exact population numbers, dates, and then compare them and explain discrepancies. The Census QuickFacts page (https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/sanfranciscocitycalifornia/INC110223) is likely the most authoritative single source.

result.title: Internal plan & target facts

details.text: Goals: (1) For three authoritative sources (government, news outlet, research institution) extract: population figure for San Francisco, date of the data, and a direct citation URL. (2) Identify discrepancies and state which is most likely accurate with justification. Likely government sources: U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts (annual estimates), California Department of Finance (DOF) population estimates, San Francisco Planning Department. Likely news outlets: San Francisco Chronicle, New York Times, SF Gate. Likely research institutions: Public Policy Institute of California (PPIC), Brookings, Population Reference Bureau (PRB), UC Berkeley research centers. Verification steps: 1) For each source, run web search to find the relevant page (search). 2) Visit the top result(s) to extract the exact population figure, the date (e.g., '2023 estimate' or 'as of July 1, 2023'), and the URL (visit required). 3) Record methodology notes if present (estimate vs. census count). 4) Compare dates and methods to explain discrepancies and choose the most likely accurate figure (prefer most recent official government estimate like U.S. Census Bureau or state DOF; note differences if news/research cite older or different-estimate figures). Measurable outputs per source: {population_figure, data_date, citation_url, source_name, methodology_note}. This reduces uncertainty by ensuring each claim is backed by a visited citation and explicit data date/method.

Goals: (1) For three authoritative sources (government, news outlet, research institution) extract: population figure for San Francisco, date of the data, and a direct citation URL. (2) Identify discrepancies and state which is most likely accurate with justification. Likely government sources: U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts (annual estimates), California Department of Finance (DOF) population estimates, San Francisco Planning Department. Likely news outlets: San Francisco Chronicle, New York Times, SF Gate. Likely research institutions: Public Policy Institute of California (PPIC), Brookings, Population Reference Bureau (PRB), UC Berkeley research centers. Verification steps: 1) For each source, run web search to find the relevant page (search). 2) Visit the top result(s) to extract the exact population figure, the date (e.g., '2023 estimate' or 'as of July 1, 2023'), and the URL (visit required). 3) Record methodology notes if present (estimate vs. census count). 4) Compare dates and methods to explain discrepancies and choose the most likely accurate figure (prefer most recent official government estimate like U.S. Census Bureau or state DOF; note differences if news/research cite older or different-estimate figures). Measurable outputs per source: {population_figure, data_date, citation_url, source_name, methodology_note}. This reduces uncertainty by ensuring each claim is backed by a visited citation and explicit data date/method.

Plan reconciliation approach: define 'current population', comparability (city vs metro), and discrepancy analysis framework

Define what counts as 'San Francisco population' (City and County boundary vs SF metro area), prioritize sources likely to be comparable (Census/official estimate year), and outline how to explain discrepancies (different vintages, ACS vs estimates, pandemic-era revisions, city vs metro). Specify final extraction fields: population figure, reference date/year, geography definition, and citation URL.

results.title: San Francisco population falls to lowest level in over a decade
results.description: By Roland Li, Yuri AvilaUpdated Jan 27, 2023 9:47 p.m. San Francisco population falls to lowest level in over a decade. Market Street sits empty of pedestrians in San Francisco in June 2022.
